title: The Argument Principle and Rouché's Theorem

epigraph.text: Even the hairs of your head are all numbered.
epigraph.quote: Zeros and poles are counted by the winding of the argument.

content.text:
The residue theorem has deep consequences for counting zeros and poles. 
The argument principle and Rouché's theorem are powerful tools in this 
direction.

content.title: Argument Principle
content.content:
Let $f$ be meromorphic inside and on a simple closed contour $\gamma$, 
with no zeros or poles on $\gamma$. If $f$ has $Z$ zeros and $P$ poles 
inside $\gamma$ (counted with multiplicity), then
\[
 \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma \frac{f'(z)}{f(z)}\,dz = Z - P.
\]
Equivalently, the change in $\arg f(z)$ as $z$ traverses $\gamma$ is 
$2\pi(Z - P)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $f$ has a zero of order $m$ at $z_0$, write $f(z) = (z-z_0)^m g(z)$ 
with $g(z_0) \neq 0$. Then
\[
 \frac{f'(z)}{f(z)} = \frac{m}{z-z_0} + \frac{g'(z)}{g(z)}.
\]
The first term contributes a residue of $m$; the second is analytic at $z_0$.

Similarly, a pole of order $n$ contributes residue $-n$.

By the Residue Theorem: $\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma \frac{f'}{f} = \sum(\text{zeros}) - \sum(\text{poles}) = Z - P$.

content.title: Rouché's Theorem
content.content:
Let $f$ and $g$ be analytic inside and on a simple closed contour $\gamma$. 
If $|g(z)| < |f(z)|$ for all $z$ on $\gamma$, then $f$ and $f + g$ have 
the same number of zeros inside $\gamma$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Consider the family $f_t = f + tg$ for $t \in [0, 1]$. On $\gamma$:
\[
 |f_t| \geq |f| - t|g| > |f| - |f| = 0.
\]
So $f_t$ has no zeros on $\gamma$ for any $t$. The number of zeros 
$Z(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma \frac{f_t'}{f_t}$ is continuous in 
$t$ and integer-valued, hence constant.

Thus $Z(0) = Z(1)$, i.e., $f$ and $f + g$ have the same number of zeros.

content.title: Another proof of the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
content.problem:
Let $p(z) = z^n + a_{n-1}z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0$ with $n \geq 1$.

Take $f(z) = z^n$ and $g(z) = a_{n-1}z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0$.

For $R$ large enough, $|g(z)| < |z|^n = |f(z)|$ on $|z| = R$.

By Rouché: $f + g = p$ has the same number of zeros as $f = z^n$, which 
has $n$ zeros (all at the origin).

Thus $p$ has exactly $n$ zeros in $\C$.
How many zeros does $z^5 + 3z + 1$ have in $|z| < 1$?
content.solution:
Let $f(z) = 3z$ and $g(z) = z^5 + 1$.

On $|z| = 1$: $|f| = 3$ and $|g| \leq |z|^5 + 1 = 2$.

Since $|g| < |f|$, by Rouché: $f + g = z^5 + 3z + 1$ has the same number 
of zeros as $f = 3z$, which has one zero at $z = 0$.

Thus the polynomial has exactly one zero in $|z| < 1$.

marginNotes.text:
The kingdom of heaven is like a grain 
of mustard seed... it is the smallest of all seeds, but when it has grown 
it is larger than all the garden plants.

marginNotes.author: Jean-Victor Poncelet
marginNotes.text:
The principle of continuity suggests 
that zeros must be preserved under perturbation.
